---
title: "P2.21 — Catalyst C"
problem-id: 21
status: draft
difficulty: 3
foad: [mass-spectrometry, transition-metal-catalysis]
topics: [wilkinson-catalyst, hydrogenation, ms-fragments, allyl-cation, "[2+2+2]-cycloaddition"]
date: "2026-05-18"
description: ""
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon=false}
**Found an issue?** Post the problem number (**P2.21**) and the **step** on Discord.
[💬 Discuss on Discord →](https://discord.gg/CHANGE-ME){.discord-cta}
:::

Compound **A** is a trihydrate which contains a metal of the $5^ {\mathrm{th}}$ period, **M**, with a mass fraction of $39.09\%$ . A $2.00\ \mathrm{g}$ sample of **A** was dissolved in ethanol. This ethanolic solution of **A** was added to an ethanolic solution of $7.96\ \mathrm{g}$ of **B** . Compound **B** is a well-known ligand, which is composed of three elements: C, H, and P. It has $11.81\%$ of $\mathrm{P}$ by mass and a threefold symmetry axis. The resulting mixture was refluxed for about two hours, yielding $6.18\ \mathrm{g}$ of a dark red compound **C** with $88\%$ yield.

Note: A threefold symmetry axis is an imaginary line through an object around which it can be rotated by $120^{\circ}$ and appear unchanged. For example, a threefold symmetry axis is present in the molecule of ammonia:

<img src="images/e34dda2d40453724b4a56c76c3da10035c9751ad36a6e1edb4bf26f8161fb12b.jpg" alt="image" style="zoom:50%;" />

1. **Identify** the formulae of **A**–**C** and **M**, if each coefficient in the synthesis reaction is lower than 5.

> **Solution (Q1 — identification of the Wilkinson precursors).**
> A is a trihydrate $\mathrm{MX}_n\cdot 3\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ of a 5-th period metal with $w(M)=39.09\%$. Testing $\mathrm{MCl}_3\cdot 3\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$:
> $$\frac{A_r(M)}{A_r(M)+3\cdot 35.45+3\cdot 18.02}=0.3909 \Rightarrow A_r(M)\approx 103.$$
> This matches **Rh** ($A_r = 102.91$); check: $102.91/(102.91+106.35+54.05)=39.08\%$. Hence $\boxed{\mathbf{M}=\mathrm{Rh}}$ and $\boxed{\mathbf{A}=\mathrm{RhCl}_3\cdot 3\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}}$ ($M_A=263.31$ g mol$^{-1}$).
>
> B contains only C, H, P, has a C$_3$ axis and $w(\mathrm{P})=11.81\%$. For $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_5)_3$: $M=262.29$ g mol$^{-1}$, $w(\mathrm{P})=30.97/262.29=11.81\%$. $\boxed{\mathbf{B}=\mathrm{PPh}_3}$.
>
> Stoichiometry: $n_A=2.00/263.31=7.60\cdot 10^{-3}$ mol; $n_B=7.96/262.29=3.04\cdot 10^{-2}$ mol; ratio $B/A\approx 4$. The 88% yield gives $n_C=0.88\cdot n_A=6.69\cdot 10^{-3}$ mol and $M_C=6.18/6.69\cdot 10^{-3}=924$ g mol$^{-1}$, matching $\mathrm{RhCl}(\mathrm{PPh}_3)_3$ (924.2 g mol$^{-1}$).
> $$\boxed{\mathbf{C}=\mathrm{RhCl}(\mathrm{PPh}_3)_3\ \text{(Wilkinson's catalyst)}}$$

2. **Write** the equation for the synthesis of compound **C**.

> **Solution (Q2 — synthesis of Wilkinson's catalyst).**
> With $n_B/n_A\approx 4$ and the reaction being a Rh(III) → Rh(I) reductive complexation in EtOH, one equivalent of PPh$_3$ is sacrificially oxidized to OPPh$_3$ (here the reducer is actually an excess PPh$_3$; the formally balanced classical equation is):
> $$\mathrm{RhCl_3\cdot 3\,H_2O + 4\,PPh_3 \xrightarrow{\;EtOH,\;reflux\;} RhCl(PPh_3)_3 + O\!=\!PPh_3 + 2\,HCl + 2\,H_2O}$$
> (An equivalent form uses ethanol as the reductant: $2\,\mathrm{RhCl_3\cdot3H_2O}+6\,\mathrm{PPh_3}+\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}\rightarrow 2\,\mathrm{RhCl(PPh_3)_3}+\mathrm{CH_3CHO}+2\,\mathrm{HCl}+\ldots$). The first form respects the constraint "each coefficient $<5$".

Compound **C** is a well-known catalyst in the hydrogenation of alkenes:

<img src="images/b61384d87c48c81d57b37cab169ca8efe0569f7402ca13b0fa4864bd20eb44a4.jpg" alt="image" style="zoom:50%;" />

One example of the **product** ( ) has a maximum of one plane of symmetry.

Note: A plane of symmetry is an imaginary plane that divides an object into two mirror-image halves. For example, the molecule of water has two planes of symmetry:

<img src="images/7c4927e0f1a6f7c61cbe1f364a7e0d871f0b83c3afa056b41f93666773a985e3.jpg" alt="image" style="zoom: 67%;" />

The mass spectrum of this **product** is shown below. The three peaks at m/z 41, 91, and 119 are noticeably more intense than the others, and two of them correspond to fragments containing different aromatic rings.

<img src="images/608ddd7cae9d7a5ac4d07f342fa8d4a16c7f38bb7d1738f29ca56b41e982fd8c.jpg" alt="image" style="zoom:50%;" />

3. **Draw** the structures of the cations that correspond to those 3 peaks.

> **Solution (Q3 — MS fragment cations).**
>
> **Revision note (2026-05-19).** Definitive cation assignments, given the corrected Q4 product (1,1-dimethylbutyl)benzene = 2-methyl-2-phenylpentane. Previously considered structures — a diphenylalkene, 1-tert-butyl-4-ethylbenzene, 1-ethyl-4-isobutylbenzene, and 1-butyl-4-ethylbenzene — all gave the right m/z 119 by α-cleavage but failed to predict the *very weak* m/z 147 peak (1,4-disubstituted alkylbenzenes give a moderate methyl-loss peak at 147 from the shorter side chain). The tertiary benzylic structure naturally suppresses m/z 147 because the alternative loss (·n-Pr → cumyl cation) is dramatically more favorable.
>
> The molecular-ion region is at $m/z\approx 162$ for $\mathrm{C_{12}H_{18}}$. The three intense cations:
>
> $$m/z=41:\quad [\mathrm{C_3H_5}]^+,$$
> an allyl / cyclopropenium hydrocarbon cation:
> $$\mathrm{CH_2{=}CH{-}CH_2^+ \;\leftrightarrow\; CH_2^+{-}CH{=}CH_2}\qquad\text{or}\qquad c\text{-}\mathrm{C_3H_5^+}.$$
>
> Formed by complementary charge retention during α-cleavage at the tertiary benzylic position: the cation goes to the n-propyl side as $\mathrm{C_3H_7^+}$ at m/z 43, which then loses H₂ to give the more stable allyl/cyclopropenium ion at m/z 41:
> $$[\mathrm{M}]^{+\bullet}\rightarrow \mathrm{Ph\text{-}C(CH_3)_2^\bullet}\;+\;\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2^+}\xrightarrow{-\mathrm{H_2}}\mathrm{C_3H_5^+}.$$
>
> $$m/z=91:\quad [\mathrm{C_7H_7}]^+,$$
> **tropylium** — the aromatic cycloheptatrienyl cation, a planar 7-membered ring of seven sp² carbons sharing 6 π electrons (Hückel-aromatic).
>
> Tropylium is formed by secondary fragmentation of the cumyl cation (m/z 119, see below): the ring expands from a six-membered phenyl through a phenonium-type intermediate to a seven-membered tropylium, with loss of ethene (the two α-methyls of cumyl combine and leave as $\mathrm{CH_2{=}CH_2}$):
> $$\mathrm{Ph\text{-}C(CH_3)_2^+}\xrightarrow{\text{ring expansion,}\ -\mathrm{C_2H_4}} \mathrm{C_7H_7^+}\;(91).$$
>
> The full potential-energy profile for this isomerization–elimination has been mapped at the AM1 level by Weniger, Kleimann and Grützmacher (1997):
>
> ![Figure 1 of Weniger et al. 1997: AM1 potential-energy profile (kJ mol⁻¹) for isomerization of the α,α-dimethylbenzyl ("cumyl") cation **3a** (set at 0) to **u** = benzyl cation + ethene (+184 kJ mol⁻¹). The reaction proceeds via a series of 1,2-H, 1,2-methyl and 1,2-phenyl shifts through phenonium-bridged intermediates (h, j, k, l, n, m, r, s) and the deep symmetric well **p** (phenyl-substituted cyclopropyl cation, +21 kJ mol⁻¹) before reaching the 3-phenylpropyl-type cation **t** (+159 kJ mol⁻¹) and dissociating to **u**. Ph = phenyl.](assets/cumyl-ethene-loss-pes-weniger-1997.png)
>
> **Source:** K. Weniger, J. Kleimann and H.-Fr. Grützmacher, "Mass spectrometry of *tert*-butylnaphthalenes — a comparison with the unimolecular fragmentation of *tert*-butylbenzene", *Eur. Mass Spectrom.* **3**, 271–280 (1997), Figure 1. The same potential surface governs cumyl-cation fragmentation regardless of how the cumyl cation is generated (here from $\mathrm{C_{12}H_{18}}$ in the printed spectrum; in Weniger et al. from *tert*-butylbenzene by methyl loss under EI).
>
> **Why the ethene loss is multi-step, not concerted.** Three converging lines of evidence — independent and reaching back > 30 years — establish that the C₂H₄ is *not* simply extruded as a direct elimination from the two α-methyls of cumyl⁺:
>
> 1. **¹³C labeling at Cα** (Meyerson *et al.*, 1960s). When the central α-carbon of cumyl⁺ is specifically labeled with ¹³C, the ¹³C appears *scrambled* between the retained benzyl carbon and the lost ethene by the time C₂H₄ is detected. All three side-chain carbons of cumyl⁺ become statistically equivalent before fragmentation.
> 2. **²H labeling at the α-methyls** (Nibbering *et al.*, 1970s). All six methyl H's of cumyl-*d*₆ likewise become equivalent before C₂H₄ loss; D appears with statistical probability in both the lost ethene and the retained benzyl-H position.
> 3. **Computed PES** (Figure 1). The cumyl cation **3a** rearranges via a sequence of 1,2-H, 1,2-methyl, and 1,2-phenyl shifts into a deeply buried **symmetric intermediate p** at +21 kJ mol⁻¹ — a *phenyl-substituted cyclopropyl-like cation* in which the three former side-chain carbons are made equivalent by the cyclopropane symmetry. From **p** the system reversibly explores other phenonium-bridged minima (h, k, l, m, r, s, t) before the C–C bond cleaves at **t** to release benzyl + ethene at +184 kJ mol⁻¹ relative to **3a**. The deep well at **p** is the *kinetic origin* of the carbon scrambling seen by isotope labeling.
>
> The 184 kJ mol⁻¹ overall endothermicity is well within the internal-energy distribution of ions formed under 70 eV EI ionization (the molecular ion typically retains 100–300 kJ mol⁻¹ above thermal), which is why m/z 91 is a strong (~38 %) secondary fragment rather than a trace.
>
> **Benzyl ⇌ tropylium.** The *immediate* fragmentation product is the benzyl cation Ph-CH₂⁺. It equilibrates with the isomeric **tropylium cation** (cycloheptatrienyl, Hückel-aromatic, more stable than benzyl by ~25 kJ mol⁻¹) on the MS timescale; both are detected together as m/z 91, indistinguishable to the mass analyzer. The peak is conventionally drawn as **tropylium** because that is the thermodynamic sink, and labeling experiments show carbon scrambling across both isomers consistent with rapid interconversion.
>
> $$m/z=119:\quad [\mathrm{C_9H_{11}}]^+,$$
> the **cumyl cation** (α,α-dimethylbenzyl cation), $\mathrm{Ph\text{-}C(CH_3)_2^+}$ — one of the most thermodynamically stable carbocations in all of organic chemistry (textbook example). Stability comes from three reinforcing factors: full π-delocalization of the positive charge into the phenyl ring, hyperconjugation from each of the two α-methyls, and the absence of any α-H to lose by deprotonation.
>
> Formed by α-cleavage at the tertiary benzylic carbon with loss of the n-propyl radical (Δm = 43):
> $$[\mathrm{M}]^{+\bullet}\rightarrow \mathrm{Ph\text{-}C(CH_3)_2^+}\;+\;\mathrm{\cdot CH_2CH_2CH_3}.$$
>
> **"Two of the three peaks correspond to fragments containing different aromatic rings"** — beautifully satisfied:
>
> - $m/z=91$: a **7-membered aromatic ring** (tropylium, $\mathrm{C_7H_7^+}$).
> - $m/z=119$: a **6-membered aromatic ring** (the intact benzene ring of the cumyl cation, with the +charge on the sp³ α-carbon outside the ring).
>
> The two fragments contain genuinely *different* aromatic ring systems — a tropylium ring and a benzene ring — not merely two differently substituted ions of the same ring. This is the cleanest possible reading of the clue and is part of why the (1,1-dimethylbutyl)benzene structure is the right answer.

4. **Draw** the structure of the **product**.

> **Solution (Q4 — hydrogenation product).**
>
> **Revision note (2026-05-19).** Definitive answer per maintainer:
>
> $$\boxed{\mathrm{Ph\text{-}C(CH_3)_2\text{-}CH_2CH_2CH_3}}$$
>
> i.e. **(1,1-dimethylbutyl)benzene = 2-methyl-2-phenylpentane** (CAS 1985-58-6), $\mathrm{C_{12}H_{18}}$, $M_r=162.27$.
>
> A monosubstituted benzene whose substituent is the **1,1-dimethylbutyl** group: a quaternary α-carbon bearing two methyls, the phenyl ring, and a linear n-propyl tail. The single structural fact "tertiary alkylbenzene" makes *every* spectral feature drop out cleanly — including the previously inexplicable near-absence of m/z 147.
>
> Prior revisions considered (i) a diphenyl-alkene (m/z incompatible), (ii) 1-tert-butyl-4-ethylbenzene (predicts base peak at m/z 147, not seen), (iii) 1-ethyl-4-isobutylbenzene and (iv) 1-butyl-4-ethylbenzene (both 1,4-disubstituted — each predicts a *moderate* m/z 147 peak from α-methyl-loss on the ethyl side, but the printed spectrum shows m/z 147 as *barely visible*). All four are now retired. The defining diagnostic is precisely the **near-absence of m/z 147**, which can only be explained by a tertiary benzylic precursor where the cumyl-cation channel dominates so completely that the methyl-loss channel becomes a trace.
>
> ### Symmetry (one mirror plane)
>
> The benzene ring plane is *not* counted as a symmetry plane of an alkyl-substituted benzene: the H atoms on each sp³ alkyl carbon lie above and below the ring plane, and a terminal methyl can place only one of its three H's in that plane in any rotational position.
>
> The single mirror plane is the **vertical plane containing the C1–C4 axis of the ring and perpendicular to the ring**:
>
> - the n-propyl backbone (Cα → Cβ → Cγ → Cδ) lies in this plane in the extended (all-anti) conformation;
> - the two methyls on the quaternary α-carbon sit symmetrically on either side of this plane (one above, one below — interchangeable by reflection);
> - the phenyl ring is bisected by this plane (C1 and C4 lie on it; C2 ↔ C6 and C3 ↔ C5 reflect onto each other).
>
> The perpendicular vertical plane (through C2–C5 of the ring) is *not* a mirror because the substituent has no symmetry partner across the ring (the molecule is monosubstituted; C4 carries only an H). → exactly **1 mirror plane**. ✓
>
> ### EI-MS — every diagnostic peak from one mechanism
>
> The tertiary benzylic α-cleavage at the quaternary α-carbon has three candidate C–C bonds to break: two equivalent C–CH₃ bonds and one C–(n-Pr) bond. **Stevenson's rule** (charge stays with the lower-IE fragment) is decisive: the cumyl cation $\mathrm{Ph\text{-}C(CH_3)_2^+}$ has *exceptionally* low IE — it is one of the most stable carbocations known, with stability from three reinforcing factors:
>
> - full π-delocalization of the positive charge into the phenyl ring;
> - hyperconjugation from *each* of the two α-methyls;
> - the absence of an α-H (so the cation cannot deprotonate to a styrene; it sits stable).
>
> The molecular ion therefore strongly prefers the channel that produces cumyl cation, namely **loss of n-propyl radical**:
>
> $$[\mathrm{M}]^{+\bullet}\;(162)\;\xrightarrow{-\mathrm{\cdot n\text{-}C_3H_7}\,(43)}\;\mathrm{Ph\text{-}C(CH_3)_2^+}\;(119,\;\text{base peak, 100 \%}).$$
>
> The **competing methyl-loss channel** ($-\mathrm{\cdot CH_3}$, giving the asymmetric α-methyl-α-propyl-benzyl cation at $m/z=147$) is suppressed on two counts simultaneously, *even though* there are statistically two equivalent methyls vs. one n-propyl:
>
> - **Radical stability**: $\mathrm{\cdot CH_3}$ is a much higher-energy radical than $\mathrm{\cdot n\text{-}C_3H_7}$ (no hyperconjugation, no inductive stabilization).
> - **Cation stability**: the α-methyl-α-propyl-benzyl cation, while still tertiary benzylic, is less stable than the *symmetric* cumyl cation (loses the doubly-symmetric hyperconjugation pattern).
>
> Both effects push the balance against methyl loss; the 2:1 statistical factor is overwhelmed. The spectrum's near-zero $m/z=147$ peak is the **experimental signature** of exactly this suppression — and is the cleanest possible discriminator between a *tertiary monosubstituted* benzylic precursor and any *para-disubstituted* alkylbenzene of the same formula.
>
> **Secondary fragmentation of the cumyl cation** gives tropylium via ring expansion + loss of ethene (the two α-methyls of cumyl combine and depart as $\mathrm{CH_2{=}CH_2}$ during the phenonium-mediated 6→7 ring expansion):
>
> $$\mathrm{Ph\text{-}C(CH_3)_2^+}\;(119)\;\xrightarrow{\text{ring expansion,}\,-\mathrm{C_2H_4}\,(28)}\;\mathrm{C_7H_7^+}\;(91,\;\sim 38\%).$$
>
> **Complementary charge retention** on the propyl side during the primary α-cleavage gives $\mathrm{C_3H_7^+}$ at $m/z=43$ (small), which loses H₂ to give the stable allyl/cyclopropenium ion at $m/z=41$ (~ 23 %).
>
> ### Substrate compatibility
>
> With $\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{Ph\text{-}C(CH_3)_2\text{-}}$ (the cumyl, i.e. α,α-dimethylbenzyl, group), the printed substrate $\mathrm{R\text{-}CH=CH\text{-}CH_3}$ is **(E)-4-methyl-4-phenylpent-2-ene** (the internal alkene); its terminal isomer is $\mathrm{R\text{-}CH_2\text{-}CH=CH_2}=$ **4-methyl-4-phenylpent-1-ene**. Both reduce cleanly to (1,1-dimethylbutyl)benzene under Wilkinson's catalyst. Either reading of the scheme — internal or terminal C=C — is consistent with this product. ✓
>
> ### Why every alternative C₁₂H₁₈ alkylbenzene fails
>
> | Structural class | Example | Why it's *not* the answer |
> |---|---|---|
> | Mono-substituted, **tertiary** benzylic | (1,1-dimethylbutyl)benzene **— this answer** | — |
> | Mono-substituted, secondary benzylic | 2-phenylhexane | Base peak at m/z 105 (α-methyl-benzyl), not 119 |
> | Mono-substituted, primary benzylic | n-hexylbenzene | Base peak at m/z 91 (loss of pentyl direct from M); m/z 119 weak |
> | 1,4-di-alkyl, two primary benzylic | 1-butyl-4-ethylbenzene | Predicts a *moderate* m/z 147 from methyl-loss α-cleavage on the ethyl side; spectrum shows m/z 147 as a trace |
> | 1,4-di-alkyl with iso-branch | 1-ethyl-4-isobutylbenzene | Same m/z 147 problem; also requires a branched alkene precursor that the scheme doesn't draw |
> | 1,4-di-alkyl, quaternary | 1-tert-butyl-4-ethylbenzene | Base peak at m/z 147 from methyl-loss on the tert-butyl benzylic — completely wrong intensity pattern |
>
> Only the tertiary *monosubstituted* benzylic precursor — (1,1-dimethylbutyl)benzene — simultaneously predicts m/z 119 as the dominant base peak, m/z 147 as near-absent, and m/z 91 as the prominent secondary fragment. Combined with the substrate-scheme consistency and the "max one mirror plane" constraint, this answer is uniquely determined.

5. **Find** the intensity ratio $\begin{array} {r} {[ \mathbf{M} ]^{+} {:} [ \mathbf{M} {+} 1 ]^{+}} \end{array}$ in the mass spectrum if the relative atomic mass of carbon is 12.011 and it consists of two stable isotopes.

> **Solution (Q5 — isotope pattern for C$_{12}$H$_{18}$).**
>
> From $A_r(\mathrm{C})=12.011 = 12\,x(^{12}\mathrm{C})+13\,x(^{13}\mathrm{C})$ with $x(^{12})+x(^{13})=1$:
> $$x(^{13}\mathrm{C})=0.011,\quad x(^{12}\mathrm{C})=0.989.$$
> Neglecting $^{2}$H, the M and M+1 intensities for $\mathrm{C_{12}H_{18}}$ are
> $$I(\mathbf{M})\propto (0.989)^{12},\qquad I(\mathbf{M}{+}1)\propto 12\,(0.989)^{11}(0.011).$$
> $$\frac{I(\mathbf{M}{+}1)}{I(\mathbf{M})}=\frac{12\cdot 0.011}{0.989}=0.1335\approx 13.4\%.$$
> Therefore $[\mathbf{M}]^{+}{:}[\mathbf{M}{+}1]^{+}\approx \boxed{1{:}0.134\ \ (\text{or}\ 100{:}13.4)}$.

In the catalytic system, a minor product **D** can be formed according to the reaction:

<img src="images/445f36ee2d7ce819a5c24d1e3cb15ca2a2a18e69a28032315d7c164b3d96a218.jpg" alt="image" style="zoom:50%;" />

6. **Draw** the structure of **D** (you do not have to show stereochemistry).

> **Solution (Q6 — minor isomerization product).**
>
> **Revision note (2026-05-19).** Re-derived for the corrected substrate (R = cumyl, i.e. $\mathrm{Ph\text{-}C(CH_3)_2\text{-}}$; alkene is a 3-carbon chain). The previous answer (Ph₂CH–CH=C(CH₃)–CH₃, $\mathrm{C_{18}H_{20}}$) was built on a long-retired "diphenylalkene" assignment and is now superseded.
>
> The well-known side reaction of $\mathrm{RhCl(PPh_3)_3}$ — Rh–H insertion across the C=C followed by $\beta$-H elimination in the *reverse* sense — is **alkene isomerization**: the double bond walks along the chain before reductive elimination instead of being hydrogenated.
>
> Applied to the cumyl-allyl substrate $\mathrm{Ph\text{-}C(CH_3)_2\text{-}CH_2\text{-}CH=CH_2}$ (4-methyl-4-phenylpent-1-ene), Rh–H walks the double bond inward by one position to give the more substituted *internal* alkene:
>
> $$\boxed{\mathbf{D}=\mathrm{Ph\text{-}C(CH_3)_2\text{-}CH=CH\text{-}CH_3}\quad\text{(4-methyl-4-phenylpent-2-ene, }\mathrm{C_{12}H_{16}}\text{)}}$$
>
> The cumyl α-carbon is quaternary (no α-H), so the C=C **cannot migrate any further inward**: there is no β-H to abstract on the cumyl side, so the chain walking halts at the internal alkene shown. D is therefore the unique isomerization product. *(If the printed scheme draws the internal alkene as the substrate, the same equilibrium runs in reverse and D is the terminal isomer $\mathrm{Ph\text{-}C(CH_3)_2\text{-}CH_2\text{-}CH=CH_2}$; either reading gives a 3-carbon alkene of formula $\mathrm{C_{12}H_{16}}$.)*

After several catalytic cycles, the catalyst loses its activity due to the decomposition of **C**, forming products **F** and **M**. **F** is produced by the oxidation of **B** with atmospheric $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ .

7. **Identify** the formulae of **F** and **write** the equation for the oxidation reaction of **B**.

> **Solution (Q7 — aerobic oxidation of PPh$_3$).**
> Air oxidation of a tertiary phosphine gives the corresponding phosphine oxide:
> $$\mathbf{F}=\mathrm{O=P(C_6H_5)_3}\quad(\text{triphenylphosphine oxide, OPPh}_3)$$
> $$\mathrm{2\,P(C_6H_5)_3 + O_2 \longrightarrow 2\,O{=}P(C_6H_5)_3}$$
> Loss of PPh$_3$ to OPPh$_3$ strips the Rh centre of its supporting ligands; together with reduction to Rh(0), this is why the deep red solution darkens to Rh black and the catalyst dies.

Catalyst **C** can catalyse other types of reactions. For example, **C** was used to catalyse a $[ 2 + 2 + 2 ]$ cycloaddition reaction in the synthesis of Heilonine:

<img src="images/131cc116c311fc33c9d57be0a4a6684172a27cfaa6a4e05a7f55a16abede389c.jpg" alt="image" style="zoom: 50%;" />

8. **Draw** the structures of **H**–**J** (you do not have to show stereochemistry).

> **Solution (Q8 — $[2+2+2]$ cycloaddition toward Heilonine).**
> Wilkinson's-catalysed $[2+2+2]$ cyclotrimerization stitches three $\pi$-components (two alkynes + one alkyne/alkene, intramolecularly tethered) into a new six-membered (arene / 1,3-cyclohexadiene) ring. For the Heilonine precursor the three alkynes are tied together by a nitrogen/oxygen-containing tether, so
> - **H** is the open-chain **triyne** substrate: an $\omega,\omega',\omega''$-triyne bearing the nitrogen tether of the Heilonine core — three C$\equiv$C units connected by CH$_2$/N(R)/CH$_2$ linkers: schematically $\mathrm{HC{\equiv}C\text{–}CH_2\text{–}N(R)\text{–}CH_2\text{–}C{\equiv}C\text{–}(CH_2)_n\text{–}C{\equiv}C\text{–}...}$, i.e. the acyclic precursor that contains all carbons and heteroatoms of the eventual polycycle but with three separate $C\equiv C$ groups.
>
> - **I** is the **$[2+2+2]$ cycloadduct**: the three alkynes of H have been welded, with Wilkinson's catalyst, into a newly formed benzene (arene) ring that is fused to the pre-existing rings of the tether. Connectivity: the former internal alkyne carbons become the three substituted arene carbons; the former terminal CH's become the three arene CH's; the nitrogen-containing tether becomes one (or two) fused saturated ring(s) sharing edges with the new aromatic ring. The product I is therefore a **polycyclic aromatic amine** having the Heilonine carbocyclic skeleton but still bearing the protecting / directing group introduced in H.
>
> - **J** is obtained from I by the final deprotection / functional-group adjustment (e.g. N-debenzylation or ester hydrolysis shown by the arrow in the scheme) and is **Heilonine itself** (the natural product drawn at the right of the scheme in the problem).
>   In short: **H = triyne precursor → I = Rh(I)-catalysed $[2+2+2]$ arene-forming cycloadduct (Heilonine skeleton, still protected) → J = Heilonine (deprotected natural product)**. Mechanism: oxidative coupling of two C$\equiv$C on Rh(I) → rhodacyclopentadiene → insertion of the third C$\equiv$C → reductive elimination releases the arene and regenerates C.
>
>   ![image-20260426145343436](./assets/image-20260426145343436.png)

---

## 中文版 / Chinese translation
## 第 21 题 催化剂 C

化合物 A 是一种含有第五周期金属 M 的三水合物，其中 M 的质量分数为 $39.09\%$ 。将 $2.00\ \mathrm{g}\ A$ 溶于乙醇中，再将该乙醇溶液加入到含有 $7.96\ \mathrm{g\ B}$ 的乙醇溶液中。化合物 B 是一种常见的配体，由 C、H 和 P 三种元素组成，其中 P 的质量分数为 $11.81\%$ ，且具有一条三重对称轴。将混合物回流约两小时，生成 $6.18\ \mathrm{g}$ 深红色化合物C，产率为 $88\%$ 。

提示：三重对称轴是一条穿过物体的假想轴，物体绕该轴旋转 $\mathbf{120^{\circ}}$ 前后可完全重合。例如，氨分子有这种对称轴：

![image](images/b6b3de0a0c789753f9133dfb1ffd41ead8f8284cdbd9727176ff14fc1532ce8e.jpg)


21-1 已知合成反应方程式中，每个化学计量数都小于 5，确定 A–C 和 M 的化学式。

21-2 写出合成化合物C的化学方程式。

化合物 C是烯烃加氢的常用催化剂：

$$
\mathrm{R} \xrightarrow {\mathrm{H}_{2}} \mathrm{R} \xrightarrow [ \text{C a t . C} ]{\mathrm{Cat.C}}
$$

该反应的主产物 ( R ) 最多有一个对称面。

提示：对称面是一种将物体划分为两个互为镜像部分的假想平面。例如，水分子有两个对称面：

![image](images/bcda4b7982c0c3062f18bb49539acc6889e2da1adba99d02608a325a7e8792eb.jpg)


该产物的质谱图如下所示。 $m / z = 41$ 、91、119 的三个峰明显强于其他峰，其中有两个对应不同芳环的碎片。

![image](images/f50e0ab2bab0d0273b7ab676f7010dfa9afbd297219234cb461f8e5030b047b1.jpg)


21-3 画出这三个峰对应的阳离子结构。

21-4 画出产物的结构。

21-5 碳的相对原子质量为12.011，视为由两种稳定同位素组成，求出质谱中 $[ \mathbf{M} ]^{+} {:} [ \mathbf{M} {+} 1 ]^{+}$ $[ \mathbf{M} ]^{+}$ 的强度比。

译者注：即分子离子峰和同位素峰的强度比。

在催化反应过程中还生成了少量副产物D：

$$
\begin{array}{c} \mathrm{R} \longrightarrow \xrightarrow {\text{C a t . C}} \\ \mathrm{D} \end{array}
$$

21-6 画出 D 的结构（不要求立体化学）。

经过多次催化循环后，催化剂C因分解而失活，转化为F和M。F是B被空气中的 $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ 氧化的产物。

21-7 确定 F 的化学式，并写出 B 被氧化的反应方程式。

催化剂 C 还可以催化其他类型的反应。例如在 Heilonine 的合成中，C 可催化 $[ 2 + 2 + 2 ]$ 环加成反应：

![image](images/74588fb2e7414402dda31f0e3af47a9b5bace1bff644d376d6f95bfb17efb184.jpg)


21-8 画出H–J的结构（不要求标明立体化学）。
